question_text: India's Planning Commission was established in March 1950 with Jawaharlal Nehru as its first chairman. Which of the following best explains the fundamental rationale for creating a centralised planning body in post-independence India?

options.A: The Planning Commission was created because the World Bank and International Monetary Fund required India to establish a central planning authority as a condition for receiving post-independence reconstruction loans
options.B: India's leaders believed that the market alone could not mobilise sufficient capital, coordinate investment across sectors, or direct resources toward social goals in a newly independent, poor country; central planning was seen as essential to achieve industrialisation, self-reliance, and poverty reduction within a democratic framework
options.C: The Planning Commission was created to replace Parliament's economic functions, allowing technocrats to make economic decisions without democratic debate or legislative approval
options.D: Central planning was mandated by Article 39 of the Indian Constitution, which made a Five Year Plan a constitutional requirement for every government to prepare and present to Parliament

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - the Planning Commission was created by India's own Cabinet resolution on March 15, 1950, without any external conditionality; the World Bank and IMF had no role in its establishment

Option C is incorrect - the Planning Commission was an advisory body that worked alongside Parliament; it did not replace Parliament's functions; budgets still required parliamentary approval and the NDC gave CMs a consultative role

Option D is incorrect - no article of the Indian Constitution mandates Five Year Plans; the Planning Commission was extra-constitutional, established by Cabinet resolution, not constitutional provision
explanation: The rationale for central planning in 1950 rested on a powerful development economics argument. Markets in a capital-scarce, infrastructure-poor, illiterate developing country would systematically underinvest in long-gestation infrastructure (power, steel, irrigation), neglect social sectors (education, health), and perpetuate colonial-era economic distortions. The state had to play a coordinating role. This 'development state' approach was globally dominant in the 1950s - not only socialist countries but also Japan, South Korea, France, and India adopted planning frameworks. For India, there was an additional imperative: foreign exchange was scarce, so investment priorities had to be set carefully to maximise self-reliance. Nehru and economist P.C. Mahalanobis believed the public sector could mobilise surplus and direct it toward heavy industry in ways private capital would not.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: The Mahalanobis model, which shaped India's Second Five Year Plan (1956-61), made a fundamental strategic choice about the structure of economic investment. Which of the following most accurately describes this model and the economic reasoning behind it?

options.A: The Mahalanobis model prioritised consumer goods and agricultural exports to earn foreign exchange, which would then be used to import capital goods for industrialisation in a later phase
options.B: The Mahalanobis model prioritised investment in heavy capital goods industries (steel, machinery, machine tools) on the argument that building domestic capacity to produce the machines that make machines would enable self-sustained growth in the long run, even if it meant slower consumption growth in the short run
options.C: The Mahalanobis model was a simple Keynesian demand stimulus - increasing government spending on infrastructure to generate employment and consumption demand, without any specific focus on industry type
options.D: The Mahalanobis model was identical to the Soviet Five Year Plan approach, involving complete nationalisation of all production and abolition of private enterprise in all sectors

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - the Mahalanobis model explicitly reversed this logic; it prioritised capital goods production over consumer goods, arguing that dependence on consumer good exports was a colonial trap; the model is famous for its 'heavy industry first' stance

Option C is incorrect - the model was not a simple Keynesian demand stimulus; it had a specific structural logic about the composition of investment (capital goods vs consumer goods), not just the aggregate level

Option D is incorrect - the Indian model retained private enterprise in consumer goods and agriculture; the public sector was reserved for heavy industry; complete nationalisation was the Soviet model, not the Indian mixed economy approach
explanation: P.C. Mahalanobis (1893-1972), director of the Indian Statistical Institute, developed a two-sector model (later four-sector) for the Second Plan. His core insight was based on a capital goods production constraint: if India invested in consumer goods industries, growth would be limited by the availability of imported machinery. If instead India built heavy industries that could produce the capital goods (machines) for future industrial expansion, it would achieve higher long-run growth despite short-run consumption sacrifice. This 'department I' (capital goods) priority over 'department II' (consumer goods) echoed Soviet planning theory but was embedded in a mixed economy framework. Steel plants (Bhilai, Rourkela, Durgapur), heavy electrical equipment, and machine tool industries were the flagship investments. Critics like B.R. Shenoy and later P.T. Bauer argued this strategy starved agriculture and consumer goods, contributing to poverty persistence.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: The First Five Year Plan (1951-56) and the Second Five Year Plan (1956-61) had different priorities. Which of the following correctly identifies the primary focus of each plan and the reasoning behind the shift?

options.A: Both plans had identical priorities focused on heavy industry; there was no significant difference in strategy between the First and Second Plans
options.B: The First Plan prioritised agriculture, irrigation, and rehabilitation of Partition refugees - addressing immediate post-independence crises; the Second Plan shifted to heavy industry and public sector investment following the Mahalanobis model, reflecting a view that the agricultural base had been stabilised and industrialisation could now accelerate
options.C: The First Plan prioritised military modernisation after the Korean War threat; the Second Plan shifted to civilian development only after the 1953 armistice reduced security concerns
options.D: The First Plan focused exclusively on foreign exchange earnings through export promotion; the Second Plan shifted to import substitution after exports failed to generate sufficient foreign exchange

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - the shift from agriculture (First Plan) to heavy industry (Second Plan) is a defining feature of early Indian planning history; calling them identical contradicts the fundamental change in development strategy

Option C is incorrect - Indian military spending was not the First Plan's primary driver; the Korean War did create some pressure but the dominant driver of First Plan priorities was food security and post-Partition rehabilitation
Option D is incorrect - neither plan focused exclusively on export promotion; India's planning framework was explicitly import-substitution oriented from the outset, not a post-failure correction
explanation: The First Five Year Plan (1951-56) was drafted under Finance Minister C.D. Deshmukh and reflected immediate post-independence priorities. India faced acute food shortages (food imports consumed scarce foreign exchange), refugee rehabilitation from Partition, and basic infrastructure deficits. Agriculture, irrigation, power, and transport received the largest allocations. The plan succeeded - food production increased significantly, and India became self-sufficient in food grains by the mid-1950s. This success created political space for the more ambitious Second Plan (1956-61), which adopted the Mahalanobis model. Heavy industry and public sector development received the lion's share. The Industrial Policy Resolution of 1956 divided industries into three schedules reserving heavy industry for the public sector. The Second Plan's capital-intensity created foreign exchange pressures that required IMF assistance in 1957-58.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: The Industrial Policy Resolution of 1956 divided Indian industries into three categories or schedules. Which of the following correctly describes this division and its significance for the mixed economy model?

options.A: Schedule A included all industries exclusively reserved for the public sector (defence, atomic energy, heavy industry); Schedule B had industries where public sector would lead but private could enter; Schedule C had industries left to private enterprise - creating a tripartite mixed economy structure
options.B: The Industrial Policy Resolution divided industries into export-oriented, import-substituting, and domestic-consumption categories, with different tax treatments for each
options.C: The resolution divided industries into large-scale (public sector only), medium-scale (both sectors), and small-scale (private only) based purely on the size of the enterprise rather than strategic importance
options.D: The 1956 Resolution nationalised all industries immediately; the three schedules referred to three five-year phases in which successive industries would be nationalised by the government

elimination_hints: Option B is incorrect - the classification was by strategic importance and ownership pattern, not by trade orientation; tax treatment was not the basis for the three-schedule division

Option C is incorrect - while size considerations existed in industrial licensing, the 1956 Resolution's three schedules were explicitly about public versus private ownership based on strategic national importance, not enterprise size

Option D is incorrect - the 1956 Resolution did not nationalise all industries; it reserved certain categories for future public sector expansion while allowing private enterprise to continue in others; full nationalisation was never the goal of India's mixed economy
explanation: The Industrial Policy Resolution of April 30, 1956 was the framework document for the Second Plan era. Schedule A: 17 industries exclusively reserved for the state, including defence, atomic energy, iron and steel, heavy machinery, railway transport, and electricity generation. Schedule B: 12 industries where the state would progressively establish new enterprises but private enterprise could also participate - including machine tools, road transport, sea transport, and fertilisers. Schedule C: All remaining industries left to private enterprise, subject to licensing. This tripartite structure operationalised the 'commanding heights of the economy' concept - the state controlling strategic infrastructure and heavy industry while private enterprise handled consumer goods, trade, and services. The resolution remained the framework for industrial policy until the 1991 liberalisation dismantled most of its restrictions.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: India's planning framework has been criticised from both the left and the right. Which of the following most accurately captures the structural critique made by economist B.R. Shenoy in his 1955 minority note to the Planning Commission, and why it proved prescient?

options.A: Shenoy's critique was that India's plans were not socialist enough; he argued that full nationalisation of all industries would produce faster growth than the mixed economy's half-measures
options.B: Shenoy argued that the Second Plan's deficit financing to fund heavy industry would be inflationary, that the public sector's inefficiency would waste capital, and that the plan's neglect of agriculture and consumer goods would create supply bottlenecks - a minority note to the Planning Commission that was overruled but proved accurate when food shortages, inflation, and foreign exchange crises emerged in the late 1950s and 1960s
options.C: Shenoy's primary critique was of India's Non-Alignment foreign policy, arguing that closer ties with the United States would bring more economic aid than the Soviet development model India was following
options.D: Shenoy argued that Five Year Plans were too short; he proposed 25-year rolling plans to provide better long-term vision, opposing the five-year cycle as politically arbitrary

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - Shenoy was a free-market economist who argued for LESS state intervention, not more; calling him a proponent of full nationalisation completely inverts his ideological position

Option C is incorrect - Shenoy's dissent was specifically about the domestic economic model (deficit financing, public sector efficiency, agricultural neglect); foreign policy was not the focus of his 1955 minority note
Option D is incorrect - Shenoy's critique was about the economic framework of the Second Plan, specifically deficit financing and public sector emphasis; plan duration was not his objection
explanation: B.R. Shenoy (1905-1978) was India's most prominent free-market economist and the lone dissenter on the Panel of Economists advising on the Second Plan. His 1955 minority note argued three main points: deficit financing would fuel inflation (since the plan was to be financed partly by running budget deficits); public sector enterprises would be inefficient due to lack of market discipline; and the neglect of consumer goods and agriculture would create shortages. All three predictions proved broadly correct. The Second Plan triggered an import surge requiring IMF standby in 1957-58. Agricultural neglect contributed to food shortages in the 1960s (requiring the Green Revolution). Public sector inefficiency became a byword for wasted capital. Shenoy's note was the intellectual foundation for the Swatantra Party's 1959 economic platform, and his arguments resurfaced in the 1991 liberalisation debate.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: The concept of a 'mixed economy' was central to India's post-independence development strategy. Which of the following best defines what 'mixed economy' meant in the Indian context of the 1950s?

options.A: A mixed economy meant that India mixed its currency - using both Indian rupees and British pounds simultaneously during a transition period after independence
options.B: A mixed economy in India meant coexistence of a dominant public sector controlling strategic industries and infrastructure alongside a private sector in consumer goods and services, both regulated by the state through licensing, price controls, and import restrictions - neither fully capitalist nor fully socialist but combining elements of both
options.C: Mixed economy referred to India's foreign trade policy of exporting to both capitalist and communist countries simultaneously, maintaining commercial relationships with both blocs during the Cold War
options.D: A mixed economy in India meant that all industries were jointly owned 50-50 by the government and private shareholders, with shared board representation in every enterprise

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - 'mixed economy' is a standard economic term referring to the ownership and control structure of productive enterprises, not a currency arrangement
Option C is incorrect - while India did trade with both blocs under Non-Alignment, 'mixed economy' is a domestic economic structure concept, not a foreign trade policy term

Option D is incorrect - no 50-50 joint ownership formula was applied across Indian industries; the public and private sectors operated separately in their respective domains under the Industrial Policy Resolution's schedule system
explanation: India's mixed economy was a distinctive post-colonial development model. The public sector occupied the 'commanding heights' - iron and steel, heavy machinery, power generation, defence production, railways, telecommunications, and later banking (after 1969 nationalisation). The private sector operated in consumer goods (textiles, sugar, food processing), trade, finance, and gradually in light engineering. The state regulated both through the Licence Raj: private investment required industrial licences, import licences for foreign exchange, and capacity licences limiting expansion. This regulatory apparatus gave the government enormous control over private enterprise without formal public ownership. The mixed economy philosophy drew from Fabian socialism (Nehru's intellectual inheritance) and from Indian industrialists' own 1944 Bombay Plan which had advocated state-led heavy industrialisation.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: The NITI Aayog replaced the Planning Commission in January 2015. Which of the following best describes the key differences between the two bodies in terms of structure, mandate, and philosophy?

options.A: NITI Aayog is identical to the Planning Commission in structure and mandate; the name change was purely cosmetic with no substantive difference in how development planning is done in India
options.B: NITI Aayog shifted from the Planning Commission's top-down directive approach (allocating resources from the Centre to states through Five Year Plans) to a think-tank and advisory model that emphasises cooperative federalism, state-specific strategies, and evidence-based policy recommendation without direct resource allocation authority
options.C: NITI Aayog has more power than the Planning Commission because it can directly impose development plans on state governments without requiring their consent, increasing central government control over state development spending
options.D: NITI Aayog was created to privatise all public sector enterprises that were established under the Planning Commission's Five Year Plans, completing the 1991 liberalisation's unfinished agenda

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - NITI Aayog explicitly does not allocate plan resources to states (that function now rests with the Finance Commission and direct ministry allocations); calling it cosmetically identical ignores this fundamental structural change
Option C is incorrect - NITI Aayog has LESS coercive authority than the Planning Commission, not more; its cooperative federalism mandate means it works with states as equal partners, not over them

Option D is incorrect - NITI Aayog's mandate is advisory and policy research; it was not created to privatise public sector enterprises; disinvestment is handled by the Ministry of Finance's DIPAM department
explanation: The Planning Commission (1950-2014) allocated Five Year Plan funds to states and central ministries, giving it enormous financial leverage over development policy. States had to present their plans to the Planning Commission for approval and resource allocation. This top-down model was criticised as centralising and not reflecting states' diverse development needs. NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India), established January 1, 2015 by Prime Minister Modi, operates as a policy think tank: it conducts research, provides analysis, facilitates inter-state learning, and advises on policy - but does not directly allocate plan resources to states. The Fourteenth Finance Commission's devolution of a larger share of taxes to states (42% of the divisible pool, up from 32%) simultaneously reduced central directed spending, making plan transfers through a Planning Commission-style body less essential.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: India's economic growth under the Nehru-era Five Year Plans was characterised by a rate that economists later called the 'Hindu rate of growth'. Which of the following most accurately defines this concept and evaluates whether it represented success or failure?

options.A: The 'Hindu rate of growth' refers to a 3.5% average annual GDP growth rate India achieved in the Nehru era; it was called this by economist Raj Krishna as a sardonic reference to its supposed inevitability - this rate was significantly below the global average for developing economies in the same period and was later seen as reflecting structural inefficiencies of the planning model
options.B: The 'Hindu rate of growth' was 8% annual GDP growth that India achieved in the Nehru era, matching the performance of East Asian tigers and reflecting the success of planned industrialisation
options.C: The term 'Hindu rate of growth' was coined by Jawaharlal Nehru himself to describe the slow, sustainable growth philosophy of Indian civilisation as opposed to the destructive capitalist growth model
options.D: The 'Hindu rate of growth' of 5% per year was actually higher than most developing countries in the 1950s-70s and is recognised by economists as a strong performance given India's initial conditions

elimination_hints: Option B is incorrect - India's Nehru-era growth rate was approximately 3.5%, not 8%; an 8% rate would have been comparable to Japan and South Korea's miracle economies, which India clearly did not match
Option C is incorrect - the term was coined by economist Raj Krishna, not Nehru; and it was explicitly sardonic and critical, not a celebration of sustainable growth philosophy

Option D is incorrect - 3.5% annual growth while population grew at about 2% meant per capita income growth of only 1.5%; this was indeed slower than comparable developing economies and insufficient to rapidly reduce poverty
explanation: Economist Raj Krishna (1924-1982) coined 'Hindu rate of growth' in 1978 as a sardonic commentary on India's seemingly inescapable slow growth trajectory. India grew at approximately 3.5% annually in the 1950s-70s, yielding per capita income growth of only about 1.5% (given ~2% population growth). This was below contemporaneous growth in South Korea (8-9%), Taiwan, and even some Latin American economies. Critics like Deepak Nayyar argue the 3.5% base established productive capacity; defenders of planning cite infrastructure and scientific institutions built. The term became shorthand for structural inefficiencies of the planning model - excessive regulation, public sector inefficiency, and insufficient agricultural investment. India's growth accelerated to 5-6% in the 1980s after partial deregulation and to 7%+ after 1991 liberalisation, suggesting the planning model's constraints were real.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: The National Development Council (NDC) was established in 1952 alongside the Planning Commission. Which of the following correctly describes the NDC's composition, role, and significance for centre-state relations in economic planning?

options.A: The NDC was a purely technical body composed of economists and statisticians without any elected representatives, tasked with evaluating the statistical validity of plan projections
options.B: The NDC included the Prime Minister, all state Chief Ministers, and Planning Commission members; it was the apex forum for approving Five Year Plans and giving states a consultative voice in national development priorities - partially mitigating the Planning Commission's centralising tendency by creating a space for federal deliberation on planning
options.C: The NDC replaced the National Finance Commission (now Finance Commission) as the mechanism for distributing central tax revenues to states, making it the most important intergovernmental fiscal transfer body
options.D: The NDC was a Parliament-approved statutory body under the National Development Act 1952, with legally binding authority to determine the plan allocations for each state regardless of the central government's preferences

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - the NDC included elected political leaders (PM and all CMs), not just technocrats; its political character was central to its function as a deliberative federal body

Option C is incorrect - the Finance Commission (constitutionally established under Article 280) handles tax devolution; the NDC handled plan approval and policy deliberation, not constitutionally mandated tax sharing

Option D is incorrect - the NDC was not established by parliamentary statute; like the Planning Commission itself, it was an executive creation without statutory basis; its decisions were not legally binding in the same way statute-backed decisions would be
explanation: The National Development Council (August 1952) was created to address the federal legitimacy problem of an extra-constitutional Planning Commission making decisions affecting states. By including all Chief Ministers alongside central government representatives, it gave the planning process a veneer of consultative federalism. In practice, the NDC's deliberations were often perfunctory - states were consulted on plans that had already been substantially drafted at the Planning Commission. However, the NDC did provide a forum for state CMs to bargain over plan allocation percentages, advocate for their regions' priorities, and occasionally secure modifications. The NDC approved all Five Year Plans and Annual Plans. Its replacement structure under NITI Aayog is the Governing Council, which has a similar composition but an advisory rather than plan-approving mandate.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: The Licence Raj was the regulatory framework accompanying India's planning model. Which of the following best describes the Licence Raj and explains how it created economic distortions despite being designed to serve planning goals?

options.A: The Licence Raj was a colonial-era British system that India inherited at independence; Nehru tried to dismantle it but was unable to because the ICS-turned-IAS bureaucracy defended their licensing powers
options.B: The Licence Raj was a system of industrial licences, import licences, capacity licences, and price controls through which the state directed private investment to plan-priority sectors; it created distortions by enabling established firms to use licences as barriers to entry against competitors, generating corruption through licence-seeking rent, and creating shortages when supply was administratively capped below demand
options.C: The Licence Raj applied only to foreign companies operating in India; domestic Indian businesses operated freely without any licensing requirements throughout the planning era
options.D: The Licence Raj was a temporary wartime rationing system introduced during the 1965 India-Pakistan War that was never dismantled, becoming a permanent feature of the economy by bureaucratic inertia

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - while colonial licensing systems existed, the post-independence Licence Raj was a new and elaborated system consciously designed to serve planning goals under the Industries Development and Regulation Act 1951; Nehru and Mahalanobis were architects, not reluctant inheritors

Option C is incorrect - the Licence Raj applied extensively to domestic Indian businesses; the Birlas, Tatas, and all other Indian industrial groups needed licences for every capacity expansion, new product line, and import
Option D is incorrect - the Licence Raj's foundation was the Industries Development and Regulation Act 1951, predating any war; it was not a wartime emergency measure
explanation: The Licence Raj operated through multiple layers: the Industries Development and Regulation Act 1951 required industrial licences for establishing or expanding any industrial undertaking above a threshold size; import licences controlled foreign exchange and restricted competing imports; capacity licences prevented firms from producing beyond licensed capacity (creating artificial scarcity); price controls in sectors like cement, steel, and fertilisers created shortages when controlled prices were below market-clearing levels. The system generated severe rent-seeking: the ability to grant or deny licences gave bureaucrats and politicians enormous discretionary power, incentivising corruption. Large established firms (the Licence Raj's 'incumbents') often used their relationships with licensing authorities to block new entrants. The Hazari Committee (1966) and Dutt Committee (1969) documented how the licensing system had become an instrument of monopolisation rather than planned development.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: Match the following key figures in India's planned development with their most significant role or contribution to the planning process.

column_b.text: Economist who coined the term 'Hindu rate of growth' in 1978 as a sardonic description of India's approximately 3.5% annual GDP growth rate under the planning model
Founder of the Indian Statistical Institute; principal architect of the Second Five Year Plan's heavy industry strategy; developed the two-sector (capital goods vs consumer goods) model
Finance Minister under Nehru; oversaw the drafting of the First Five Year Plan focusing on agriculture, irrigation, and post-Partition rehabilitation; later resigned over Budget-related disagreements

Free-market economist; submitted a minority dissent note to the Planning Commission in 1955 predicting that deficit financing and heavy industry priority would cause inflation and foreign exchange crises

elimination_hints: P.C. Mahalanobis is uniquely associated with the 'two-sector model', the Second Five Year Plan, and the Indian Statistical Institute - only option Q combines all three of these distinguishing features

B.R. Shenoy's defining moment is the 1955 minority dissent note to the Planning Commission predicting inflation and foreign exchange crises - option S uniquely identifies both the minority note and these specific predictions
Raj Krishna's unique contribution is coining 'Hindu rate of growth' in 1978 with the 3.5% figure - option P is the only one that uses this specific term and the 1978 date
explanation: P.C. Mahalanobis (1893-1972), the statistical genius behind the Second Plan, built his model on the insight that capital goods investment would unlock self-sustained industrial growth. C.D. Deshmukh (1896-1982) was India's first Indian-born Finance Minister and oversaw the First Plan; he resigned in 1956 over the States Reorganisation of Bombay. B.R. Shenoy (1905-1978) was the lone dissenter among the Second Plan's economists; his warnings proved accurate. Raj Krishna (1924-1982) coined 'Hindu rate of growth' at a seminar in 1978, using it to describe the structural limitations of the planning model that had constrained India to approximately 3.5% annual growth for three decades.
concept_tags: C.D. Deshmukh First Plan Finance Minister
Raj Krishna Hindu rate of growth
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: Match the following Five Year Plans or planning-related policies with their correct description of primary focus, associated economic strategy, and key outcome or controversy.

column_b.text: Three annual plans replacing the Five Year Plan framework; necessitated by the foreign exchange crisis, drought, war (1965 Pakistan, 1962 China), and the need for IMF stabilisation conditions that disrupted plan continuity
Mahalanobis model; heavy industry and public sector priority; Bhilai/Rourkela/Durgapur steel; triggered foreign exchange crisis requiring IMF standby assistance in 1957-58
Aimed at self-sufficiency in food and defence; fell short due to two wars and two droughts; became the plan that 'failed', discrediting the planning framework's optimistic projections
Agriculture, irrigation, power, transport; rehabilitation of Partition refugees; drew on Harrod-Domar growth model; exceeded targets; food production increased significantly

elimination_hints: The First Plan's defining feature is 'agriculture, irrigation, rehabilitation' and 'exceeded targets' - only option S describes both the agricultural focus and the successful outcome; the Harrod-Domar model reference is an additional distinguishing detail

The Second Plan's defining feature is 'Mahalanobis model', 'heavy industry', and the 'foreign exchange crisis/IMF standby' - option Q uniquely combines the Mahalanobis model, the three steel plants, and the 1957-58 IMF assistance

The Plan Holiday's defining feature is 'three annual plans' replacing the Five Year Plan framework due to wars and drought - option P uniquely describes both the annual plan substitution and lists all the crises (1965 war, 1962 war, drought, IMF conditions)
explanation: First Plan (1951-56): Agriculture-first, exceeded growth targets, food production rose - used Harrod-Domar framework. Second Plan (1956-61): Mahalanobis model, heavy industry flagship, triggered 1957-58 foreign exchange crisis requiring IMF assistance. Third Plan (1961-66): Ambitious targets for self-sufficiency but caught between the 1962 China war, 1965 Pakistan war, and severe droughts of 1965-66 - the most conspicuous plan failure. Plan Holiday (1966-69): Three successive Annual Plans replaced the Fourth Plan due to the cumulative crises; this period coincided with the Green Revolution's introduction of High Yielding Varieties, the 1966 rupee devaluation, and the policy uncertainty of the Syndicate-Indira struggle.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: Match the following economic concepts or policies from India's planning era with their correct definition and role in the planned development framework.

column_b.text: Legislation restricting foreign companies from holding majority equity in Indian subsidiaries; part of the self-reliance framework; led to Coca-Cola and IBM exiting India in 1977 rather than diluting their stakes

Government enterprises in strategic sectors (steel, power, petrochemicals, banking) - meant to capture investible surplus and direct it to plan priorities; criticised for low productivity, overstaffing, and absorbing subsidies rather than generating profits

Government financing development spending by printing currency or borrowing from the Reserve Bank rather than through taxation; intended to mobilise investment in the short term but criticised by Shenoy for triggering inflation

Development strategy of producing domestically the manufactured goods previously imported, reducing import dependence; India's planning model relied on this as the path to industrial self-reliance and foreign exchange conservation

elimination_hints: Import Substitution Industrialisation's definition is specifically 'producing domestically what was previously imported' to reduce import dependence - only option S captures both the domestic production element and the foreign exchange conservation rationale

Deficit financing's definition involves 'printing currency or borrowing from the Reserve Bank' rather than taxation - option R uniquely identifies the Reserve Bank borrowing mechanism and connects it to Shenoy's inflation prediction
FERA 1973's defining feature is the Coca-Cola and IBM exit from India in 1977 after refusing to dilute their equity - option P is the only one containing this specific historical detail
explanation: Import Substitution Industrialisation (ISI) was the dominant development strategy globally in the 1950s-70s: protect domestic infant industries from foreign competition, build domestic manufacturing capacity, and reduce import dependence. India's ISI was implemented through high tariff walls, import licensing, and FERA restrictions on foreign investment. Public Sector Undertakings grew from a handful in 1947 to over 200 by the 1980s, employing millions but often requiring operating subsidies. Deficit financing was central to Second Plan funding - the monetised deficit was estimated at about 12% of total plan expenditure, contributing to the 1957-58 inflation. FERA (1973) required foreign companies to dilute their Indian subsidiaries to 40% foreign equity; Coca-Cola chose to leave India in 1977, not returning until 1993 after liberalisation.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text:
Assertion (A): India's planning model successfully achieved its primary goal of creating a diversified industrial base and reducing the country's dependence on manufactured imports, making the planning era an unambiguous success despite its acknowledged inefficiencies.
Reason (R): By the 1970s, India had established steel plants, engineering industries, petrochemical facilities, and a scientific research infrastructure that would not have emerged from private market investment alone, providing the industrial foundation for subsequent economic acceleration.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

explanation: The Assertion is false as stated: calling it 'unambiguous success despite inefficiencies' overstates the outcome. The planning model produced a mixed record - the industrial base was indeed built (R is true), but the 'Hindu rate of growth' at 3.5% meant poverty reduction was painfully slow, millions remained poor, food insecurity persisted until the Green Revolution, and the 1991 foreign exchange crisis (requiring IMF bailout) revealed fundamental structural weaknesses. Calling this 'unambiguous success' contradicts the assessment of most development economists including Jagdish Bhagwati, T.N. Srinivasan, and even Manmohan Singh (who called the 1991 crisis partly a product of planning era distortions). The Reason is true: the steel, engineering, and scientific infrastructure built in the planning era did provide foundations for post-1991 growth. Since A overclaims success while R accurately describes one real achievement, the answer is D.
concept_tags: industrial base built but poverty slow
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text:
Assertion (A): The Planning Commission was a constitutional body established under Article 280 of the Indian Constitution with legally binding authority over economic policy-making.
Reason (R): The Finance Commission, which is the constitutionally mandated body under Article 280, performs a different function (devolution of central taxes to states) and has different membership and frequency of operation than the Planning Commission did.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

explanation: The Assertion is false: the Planning Commission was NOT a constitutional body and was NOT established under Article 280. It was created by a Cabinet resolution on March 15, 1950 - an executive act with no constitutional basis. This extra-constitutional status was frequently criticised as a democratic deficit. The Reason is true: Article 280 of the Constitution establishes the Finance Commission (not the Planning Commission) as a constitutional body for recommending the distribution of central taxes between the Union and states. The Finance Commission is constituted every five years with different membership, while the Planning Commission had permanent membership. They served different functions: the Planning Commission allocated plan grants (discretionary development spending) while the Finance Commission distributes tax revenues under constitutional formula. Since A is false and R correctly distinguishes Finance Commission from Planning Commission, the answer is D.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text:
Assertion (A): The Mahalanobis model's prioritisation of heavy capital goods industries over consumer goods was economically irrational and had no theoretical basis, representing pure ideological preference for Soviet-style planning over market economics.
Reason (R): The theoretical basis for the Mahalanobis model's capital goods priority was the argument that domestic production of capital equipment would break the long-run constraint of depending on imported machinery for industrial expansion, enabling self-sustained growth after an initial sacrifice period.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

explanation: The Assertion is false: the Mahalanobis model was not 'pure ideology without theoretical basis'. It had a rigorous theoretical underpinning based on capital goods production constraints, foreign exchange scarcity, and the two-department model (drawing on classical economists including Marx's reproduction schemes and later formalised by Mahalanobis himself with mathematical precision). Whether the strategy was wise given India's specific conditions is debated, but dismissing it as theoretically baseless contradicts the fact that it drew on mainstream development economics of the 1950s and was accepted by respected international economists including the Soviet Gosplan advisers and initially Joan Robinson. The Reason is true: it accurately describes the model's theoretical logic. Since A is false (the model had genuine theoretical foundations) and R is true (accurately describes those foundations), the answer is D.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: Arrange the following milestones in India's planned development in the correct chronological sequence from the earliest to the most recent.
items: (i) NITI Aayog replaces the Planning Commission; cooperative federalism model replaces directive planning
(ii) First Five Year Plan launched; Planning Commission begins operation under Nehru's chairmanship
(iii) Mahalanobis model adopted for the Second Five Year Plan; Industrial Policy Resolution 1956 reserves heavy industries for public sector
(iv) Plan Holiday declared; three consecutive Annual Plans replace the Fifth Five Year Plan due to wars, droughts, and the foreign exchange crisis
(v) Avadi Congress Resolution declares the 'socialist pattern of society' as the national economic goal, giving ideological grounding to the planning model
elimination_hints: The Planning Commission and First Five Year Plan launch (1950-51) is clearly the earliest event in this sequence - the institution must be established before any subsequent resolutions or plans can reference it
The Avadi Resolution (1955) came after the Planning Commission's establishment but before the Second Plan (1956-61) - it provided the ideological rationale that the Second Plan operationalised
NITI Aayog (January 2015) is the most recent event; the Plan Holiday (1966-69) is much earlier; these two provide the clear first and last anchors of the latter part of the sequence
explanation: Planning chronology: (ii) 1950-51 - Planning Commission established, First Plan launched; (v) January 1955 - Avadi Congress Resolution declares socialist pattern of society; (iii) 1956-61 - Second Plan with Mahalanobis model, Industrial Policy Resolution April 1956; (iv) 1966-69 - Plan Holiday: three Annual Plans replace the stalled Fourth Plan after the 1962/1965 wars, droughts, and foreign exchange crisis; (i) January 1, 2015 - NITI Aayog replaces Planning Commission. This sequence spans 65 years of India's planned development trajectory from establishment to transformation.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: Arrange the following steps in the correct logical sequence showing how a private industrial investment decision was processed through India's Licence Raj framework under the planning model.
items: (i) The entrepreneur actually begins construction and production at the licensed capacity, contributing to the plan's targeted output in that sector
(ii) An entrepreneur identifies an investment opportunity in, say, a new cement plant and prepares a business proposal

(iii) The entrepreneur applies for an industrial licence from the Ministry of Industry, which evaluates whether the investment is consistent with the plan's sectoral targets and whether the applicant has adequate technical and financial capacity
(iv) The entrepreneur applies for an import licence to bring in machinery and raw materials not available domestically, consuming a portion of the plan's allocated foreign exchange for that sector
(v) The entrepreneur applies to a nationalised bank (post-1969) for term loans, where the bank's lending committee evaluates creditworthiness and the investment's consistency with plan priorities
elimination_hints: The entrepreneur identifying the opportunity and preparing a proposal (item ii) is logically the first step - no applications can be filed before the business idea exists and is formulated into a proposal

The industrial licence application (item iii) must come before financing (item v) and import licence (item iv) - without a confirmed industrial licence, a bank would not provide term loans and import licences would not be sanctioned for an unlicensed project
Actual production (item i) is the final step - construction and production can only begin after all licences are secured and financing is arranged
explanation: The Licence Raj's procedural sequence illustrates its complexity. First, an entrepreneur conceptualises a project (ii). Then comes the industrial licence application to the licensing committee under IDRA 1951 (iii) - this could take months or years, involve multiple revisions, and require significant lobbying. Once an industrial licence was in hand, the entrepreneur could approach a financial institution for term loans (v) - since 1969, this meant dealing with nationalised banks whose lending criteria included plan consistency. With financing confirmed, the entrepreneur applied for import licences for capital equipment and raw materials not available domestically (iv). Only after all these approvals would actual production begin (i). The entire process could take years, creating the 'inspection licence permit raj' that Rajagopalachari criticised.
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text:
Examine the following statements about India's planning model and Five Year Plans, then identify which combination is correct:
(i) The Planning Commission was established under Article 280 of the Indian Constitution as a constitutional body with legally binding authority over India's economic policy.
(ii) The Mahalanobis model that shaped India's Second Five Year Plan prioritised heavy capital goods industries on the theoretical argument that domestic capital goods production would enable self-sustained long-run industrial growth.
(iii) The 'Plan Holiday' of 1966-69 consisted of three Annual Plans that replaced the Fourth Five Year Plan due to the disruptions caused by the 1965 Indo-Pakistan War, droughts, foreign exchange crisis, and IMF stabilisation conditions.
(iv) NITI Aayog, which replaced the Planning Commission in January 2015, continues to allocate Five Year Plan funds directly to state governments as the Planning Commission did.
explanation: Statement (i) is FALSE - the Planning Commission was established by Cabinet resolution on March 15, 1950, not under Article 280; Article 280 establishes the Finance Commission, a different body. Statement (ii) is TRUE - the Mahalanobis capital goods priority model is accurately described. Statement (iii) is TRUE - the Plan Holiday description is accurate. Statement (iv) is FALSE - NITI Aayog specifically does NOT allocate plan resources to states; that function was dissolved when the Planning Commission was replaced; NITI Aayog is a think tank and advisory body, not a resource-allocating body; Five Year Plans as such were discontinued. Only (ii) and (iii) are correct.
concept_tags: Planning Commission Cabinet resolution not Article 280
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science

question_text: The table below compares India's first five Five Year Plans across dimensions of primary sector focus, growth target, actual growth achieved, key investment, and major problem encountered. Based on a careful reading of the patterns across all five plans, which conclusion is most strongly supported by the comparative data?

options.A: The data shows a consistent pattern of all five plans achieving or exceeding their growth targets, demonstrating the unambiguous success of India's centrally planned development model
options.B: The data reveals a pattern of declining plan performance from the Second Plan onward: the First Plan's agricultural focus succeeded, but the shift to heavy industry created structural vulnerabilities (foreign exchange pressure, agricultural neglect, dependence on external shocks) that caused the Third Plan to fail badly and the Plan Holiday to occur - suggesting the Mahalanobis strategy's ambitious assumptions were repeatedly tested and found wanting by external shocks that planning could not anticipate or buffer
options.C: The data shows that plans with higher growth targets always performed better than plans with modest targets, confirming that ambitious planning produces better outcomes than conservative planning
options.D: The data shows that foreign aid and Soviet assistance were the decisive factors determining plan success, with plans that received more Soviet assistance performing better regardless of their sectoral priorities

elimination_hints: Option A is incorrect - the Third Plan clearly failed to meet its targets (wars and droughts disrupted it severely) and the Plan Holiday shows the planning system's breakdown; calling all five plans successful contradicts the data

Option C is incorrect - the Second Plan had ambitious targets and ran into a foreign exchange crisis; the Third Plan had ambitious targets and failed due to external shocks; higher ambition did not guarantee better performance

Option D is incorrect - Soviet assistance (Bhilai Steel Plant) was an input to the Second Plan but cannot explain the First Plan's success (which predated major Soviet assistance) or the Third Plan's failure
explanation: The five-plan comparative data supports the structural vulnerability interpretation. The First Plan succeeded because its agricultural focus produced tangible results quickly with limited import dependence. The Second Plan's heavy industry shift created multiple vulnerabilities: foreign exchange dependence for capital goods imports triggered the 1957-58 IMF crisis; agricultural neglect contributed to food shortages. The Third Plan, which tried to combine agriculture and industry targets, was devastated by two wars and two droughts - crises that the planning framework had no buffer for. The Plan Holiday (1966-69) was the system's acknowledgment that the rigid Five Year Plan framework could not accommodate major external shocks. The pattern suggests a systematic limitation: heavy industry-led planned development requires stable external conditions that a poor, vulnerable economy cannot guarantee. This analysis aligns with the critique advanced by Jagdish Bhagwati and T.N. Srinivasan in 'India: Planning for Industrialisation' (1970).
topic: Planning Commission and Five Year Plans
subject: political-science
